elements.sent-2411-002: Simultaneous Transmitting and Reflecting Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (STAR-RISs) are being explored for the next generation of sixth-generation (6G) networks.
elements.sent-2411-003: A promising configuration for their deployment is within cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, despite the advantages that STAR-RISs could bring, challenges such as electromagnetic interference (EMI) and phase errors may lead to significant performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we investigate the impact of EMI and phase errors on STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems and propose techniques to mitigate these effects.
elements.sent-2411-006: We introduce a novel projected gradient descent (GD) algorithm for STAR-RIS coefficient matrix design by minimizing the local channel estimation normalised mean square error.
elements.sent-2411-007: We also derive the closed-form expressions of the uplink and downlink spectral efficiency (SE) to analyze system performance with EMI and phase errors, in which fractional power control methods are applied for performance improvement.
elements.sent-2411-008: The results reveal that the projected GD algorithm can effectively tackle EMI and phase errors to improve estimation accuracy and compensate for performance degradation with nearly $10\%\sim20\%$ SE improvement.
elements.sent-2411-009: Moreover, increasing access points (APs), antennas per AP, and STAR-RIS elements can also improve SE performance.
elements.sent-2411-010: Applying STAR-RIS in the proposed system achieves a larger $25\%$-likely SE than conventional RISs.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, the advantages of employing more STAR-RIS elements are reduced when EMI is severe.
elements.sent-2411-012: In past developments, massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) has been one of the cornerstone technologies for enhancing data rates, reliability and coverage\cite{10297571,10225319,10167480 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, in cellular networks with cell boundaries, massive MIMO experiences harsh inter-cell interference\cite{9665300 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: To address the limit of massive MIMO in cellular networks, cell-free massive MIMO has been proposed to help diminish inter-cell interference, provide more extensive coverage and leverage favorable propagation configurations\cite{10058895,10422885,10535986 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Cell-free massive MIMO utilizes a distributed architecture with a central processing unit (CPU) that facilitates numerous geographically distributed access points (APs), which can synchronously provide service to users\cite{7917284,9416909,9665300 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Cell-free massive MIMO integrates distributed networks and massive MIMO, so that the spectral efficiency (SE) performance can be greatly enhanced, even while using conjugate beamforming\cite{7827017,10297571,qian2024impactchannelagingelectromagnetic .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In \cite{7917284,10167480 , a CPU-based large-scale fading decoding (LSFD) receiver has been proposed for uplink SE performance enhancement to utilize the benefits of cell-free massive MIMO.}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, more APs introduce higher network overhead and power consumption, and the proposed system can experience poor quality of service (QoS) under severe propagation conditions \cite{9665300,10167480,10058895 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Therefore, advanced technologies must be developed to meet the required QoS and handle these challenges in cell-free massive MIMO.
elements.sent-2411-020: Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RISs) can be used to shape electromagnetic waves smartly without increasing the number of APs or power consumption.
elements.sent-2411-021: They are thus regarded as a promising technology to assist cell-free massive MIMO systems \cite{9326394,10058895,10297571 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: RISs are two-dimensional structures containing numerous passive reflecting elements that have the potential to reshape the propagation environment using the amplitude and phase shift adjustment at the RIS surface.
elements.sent-2411-023: RISs also do not need digital signal processing or active power amplifiers, so they can be implemented with lower complexity than a regular massive MIMO base station \cite{qian2024impactchannelagingelectromagnetic,9905943,9875036 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: RISs can enhance wireless network performance by introducing additional controllable and reconfigurable cascaded links with low cost and power consumption\cite{9665300,10225319,9863732 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Integrating RIS with cell-free massive MIMO could allow the advantages of both to be obtained jointly\cite{9322151,10001167,10556753 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: For example, the SE performance of spatially correlated RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems has been investigated \cite{10001167,10264149 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: In \cite{10225319,9322151,10621117 , channel estimation improvement methods were introduced.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Moreover, \cite{10167480,10621117 evaluated the system performance of electromagnetic interference (EMI)-aware RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO and \cite{qian2024impactchannelagingelectromagnetic studied the performance of RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems with channel aging and EMI.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In their conventional form, RIS is configured to reflect incoming signals, and therefore, in wireless configurations, the receiver and transmitter are located on the same side of the RIS\cite{10316600,10297571 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: However, in practice, users may be positioned on both RIS sides \cite{9570143,10297571 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: To tackle this limitation, \cite{9437234,9690478 introduced the novel simultaneously transmitting and reflecting RIS (STAR-RIS) concept.}
elements.sent-2411-032: STAR-RISs can provide full space coverage since the incoming signals incident on the STAR-RIS could be separated into reflected and transmitted signals \cite{9570143,10297571 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Moreover, the amplitudes and phases of the reflected and transmitted signals can be controlled by reflection and transmission coefficients, respectively, of the STAR-RIS \cite{9570143,9786058,10297571 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: To realize the benefits of STAR-RISs, \cite{9462949 investigated the fundamental coverage characterization.}
elements.sent-2411-035: STAR-RIS was also introduced in \cite{10001603 to enhance full-duplex communication system performance.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Motivated by the interplay of RISs and cell-free massive MIMO systems, the joint benefits of STAR-RISs and cell-free massive MIMO systems may also be exploited \cite{10297571 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Research on STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems is at an early stage.
elements.sent-2411-038: It includes \cite{10297571 , which introduced spatial correlation, and \cite{10316600 , which studied the sum-rate optimization of STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Similar to conventional RIS-assisted systems \cite{10167480,10621117,10133717,9598875 , when STAR-RIS elements are highly correlated, EMI impinging on the STAR-RIS will degrade system performance.}
elements.sent-2411-040: However, no existing analyses focus on EMI-aware STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems.
elements.sent-2411-041: This includes the absence of the analysis of performance limits and the introduction of design guidelines for STAR-RIS-assisted networks suffering from EMI.
elements.sent-2411-042: Moreover, as far as we know, the study of STAR-RIS/RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO with phase errors has also been limited with \cite{9786058,10373089 introducing the non-negligible phase error effect on STAR-RIS-assisted massive MIMO systems.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Motivated by these observations, it is useful to analyze the joint effect of EMI and phase errors on the performance of STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems over spatially correlated channels.
elements.sent-2411-044: To the best of our knowledge, this paper is the first to focus on the performance analysis of spatially correlated STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems, where STAR-RISs are non-ideal and experience EMI and phase errors.
elements.sent-2411-045: We derive the data transmission model and the closed-form expressions of the uplink and downlink SE with corresponding fractional power control.
elements.sent-2411-046: We introduce a projected gradient descent (GD) algorithm to enhance the channel estimation normalised mean square error (NMSE) and reduce the performance degradation caused by EMI and phase errors.
elements.sent-2411-047: Uplink transmission utilizes the LSFD at CPU and maximum ratio (MR) at APs.
elements.sent-2411-048: Additionally, downlink transmission applies conjugate beamforming.
elements.sent-2411-049: We also evaluate the sum SE performance with fractional power control methods.
elements.sent-2411-050: In the following, we summarize the major contributions of this work as: • We establish a spatially correlated STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO model with EMI and phase errors.
elements.sent-2411-051: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to introduce EMI and phase errors in STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO.
elements.sent-2411-052: We then conduct Monte Carlo simulations to validate the closed-form analytical results.
elements.sent-2411-053: Our proposed GD algorithm can minimize the channel estimation NMSE and introduce extra channel estimation accuracy gain.
elements.sent-2411-054: Moreover, more STAR-RIS elements are required to improve system performance, while STAR-RISs cannot bring significant gain when EMI is severe.
elements.sent-2411-055: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-056: Section II introduces the spatially correlated STAR-RIS-assisted channel model with EMI and phase errors.
elements.sent-2411-057: Section III introduces uplink channel estimation with pilot contamination and the projected GD algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-058: Section IV and Section V derive closed-form analytical expressions for the uplink and downlink SE with fractional power control, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-059: We provide numerical results and discussions in Section VI, and Section VII summarizes the paper and proposes subsequent works.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Gradient Descent Based STAR-RIS Design} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \renewcommand{\algorithmicrequire}{Inputs:} \Require $\epsilon$ (tolerance), IterMax; \renewcommand{\algorithmicensure}{Output:} \Ensure ${\boldsymbol{\theta}}$, $u$, $\text{NMSE}({\boldsymbol{\theta}},u)$ \State Initialize ${\boldsymbol{\theta}}^0$, $u^0$, $f^0=\text{NMSE}({\boldsymbol{\theta}}^0,u^0)$ \For{$\text{t}= 1 :\text{IterMax}$} \State ${\boldsymbol{\theta}}=[]$, $u=[]$; \State $\mu=1-\displaystyle\frac{\text{t}-1}{2\cdot\text{IterMax}}$;~~~~(step size) \State ${\boldsymbol{\theta}}=\text{P}_{{\boldsymbol{\theta}}}\left({\boldsymbol{\theta}}^0-\mu\nabla _{{\boldsymbol{\theta}}^0}\text{NMSE}({\boldsymbol{\theta}}^0,u^0)\right)$ \State $u=\text{P}_{u}\left(u^0-\mu\nabla _{u^0}\text{NMSE}({\boldsymbol{\theta}}^0,u^0)\right)$ \State Calculate $f=\text{NMSE}({\boldsymbol{\theta}},u)$ \If {$\big{|}f-f^0\big{|}\leq \epsilon$} \State $break$ \Else \State ${\boldsymbol{\theta}}^0={\boldsymbol{\theta}}$, $u^0=u$, $f^0=f$; \EndIf \EndFor \end{algorithmic} \label{Algorithm}

elements.sent-2411-195: Fig. \ref{fig_6 indicates the sum SE as a function of the number of STAR-RIS elements.}
elements.sent-2411-196: We can find that increasing the number of STAR-RIS elements could significantly enhance the system performance with a gradually decreasing growth trend.
elements.sent-2411-197: Moreover, we can find that smaller inter-element distance introduces higher spatial correlation to decrease the system performance.
elements.sent-2411-198: A remarkable insight is that more STAR-RIS elements increase the performance of the MS protocol, surpassing that of the ES protocol under harsh propagation conditions.
elements.sent-2411-199: This is because more STAR-RIS elements will introduce more severe EMI and phase errors to degrade the system performance.
elements.sent-2411-200: The MS protocol might introduce higher energy in the area where users are close to the APs to maintain performance.
elements.sent-2411-201: To further show the benefits of STAR-RIS deployment, we present a benchmark scenario with a conventional transmitting-only RIS and a reflecting-only RIS positioned next to each other at the exact STAR-RIS location.
elements.sent-2411-202: For fairness, each conventional RIS (cRIS) is assumed to have $L/2$ elements \cite{9570143 .}
elements.sent-2411-203: It shows that the proposed STAR-RIS-assisted system can achieve a $25\%$-likely SE improvement than that with cRISs, which motivates the deployment of STAR-RISs in cell-free massive MIMO systems.
elements.sent-2411-204: Appropriately increasing the number of STAR-RIS elements can improve system performance with EMI and phase errors.
elements.sent-2411-205: Meanwhile, EMI elimination and phase error compensation methods should be introduced to enhance the benefits of deploying more STAR-RIS elements.
elements.sent-2411-206: STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO represents a sustainable and attractive architecture that leverages their combined advantages.
elements.sent-2411-207: To our knowledge, this work is the first to analyze the performance of spatially correlated STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems, taking into account the impacts of EMI and phase errors while conducting SE performance analysis.
elements.sent-2411-208: We introduced the projected GD algorithm for designing the STAR-RIS coefficient matrix, aimed at minimizing the NMSE.
elements.sent-2411-209: We derived the closed-form expressions of the uplink and downlink SE with respective uplink LSFD decoding and downlink conjugate beamforming for performance evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-210: The results reveal that EMI and phase errors cause non-negligible degradation in channel estimation accuracy and sum SE.
elements.sent-2411-211: Our proposed projected GD algorithm can improve estimation accuracy and introduce a $ 10\%\sim20\%$-likely SE improvement.
elements.sent-2411-212: Increasing APs/AP antennas and STAR-RIS elements can also reduce performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-213: However, severe EMI could mitigate the advantages of increasing STAR-RIS elements.
elements.sent-2411-214: More users might introduce severe inter-user interference and pilot contamination to reduce the average SE per user.
elements.sent-2411-215: Distinct from the conventional insight that the ES protocol outperforms the MS protocol with increasing APs/AP antennas, the MS protocol can outperform the ES protocol under harsh propagation conditions with increasing STAR-RIS elements.
elements.sent-2411-216: Moreover, STAR-RIS achieves a larger $25\%$-likely SE than conventional RISs, underscoring the necessity of deploying STAR-RISs in cell-free massive MIMO.
elements.sent-2411-217: Inspired by these results, our future work will focus on scalable implementation, advanced channel estimation schemes, inter-user interference and EMI mitigation methods to sufficiently realize the advantages of deploying STAR-RISs in the cell-free massive MIMO to serve more users.. \ifCLASSOPTIONcaptionsoff \fi \begin{appendices.}

elements.sent-2411-238: Simultaneous Transmitting and Reflecting Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (STAR-RISs) are being explored for sixth-generation (6G) wireless networks.
elements.sent-2411-239: We introduce a tailored projected gradient descent (GD) algorithm for STAR-RIS coefficient matrix design by minimizing the local channel estimation normalized mean square error (NMSE).
elements.sent-2411-240: We also derive the novel closed-form expressions of the uplink and downlink spectral efficiency (SE) to analyze system performance with EMI and phase errors, in which fractional power control methods are introduced for performance improvement.
elements.sent-2411-241: The results reveal that the projected GD algorithm can effectively tackle EMI and phase errors to improve estimation accuracy and compensate for performance degradation with nearly $30\%$ NMSE improvement and over $10\%$ SE improvement.
elements.sent-2411-242: Moreover, increasing the number of access points (APs), antennas per AP, and STAR-RIS elements can also improve SE performance.
elements.sent-2411-243: However, the advantages of employing STAR-RIS are reduced when EMI and phase errors are severe.
elements.sent-2411-244: Notably, compared to conventional RISs, the incorporation of STAR-RIS in the proposed system yields better performance and presents less performance degradation in highly impaired environments.
elements.sent-2411-245: To address the limit of massive MIMO in cellular networks, cell-free massive MIMO has been proposed to help diminish inter-cell interference, provide more extensive coverage and leverage favorable propagation configurations \cite{10058895,10422885,10535986 .}
elements.sent-2411-246: Cell-free massive MIMO utilizes a distributed architecture with a central processing unit (CPU) that facilitates numerous geographically distributed access points (APs), which can synchronously provide service to users \cite{7917284,9416909,9665300 .}
elements.sent-2411-247: Cell-free massive MIMO integrates distributed networks and massive MIMO, so that the spectral efficiency (SE) performance can be greatly enhanced, even while using conjugate beamforming \cite{7827017,10297571,10621117 .}
elements.sent-2411-248: RISs also do not need digital signal processing or active power amplifiers, so they can be implemented with lower complexity than a regular massive MIMO base station \cite{10621117,9905943,9875036 .}
elements.sent-2411-249: RISs can enhance wireless network performance by introducing additional controllable and reconfigurable cascaded links with low cost and power consumption \cite{9665300,10225319,9863732 .}
elements.sent-2411-250: Integrating RIS with cell-free massive MIMO could allow the advantages of both to be obtained jointly, attracting research interests \cite{9322151,10001167,10556753 .}
elements.sent-2411-251: Thus, the SE performance of spatially correlated RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems has been investigated \cite{10001167,10264149 .}
elements.sent-2411-252: Moreover, \cite{10167480,10621117 evaluated the system performance of electromagnetic interference (EMI)-aware RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO and \cite{10621117 introduced channel estimation improvement methods to mitigate the adverse effects of EMI.}}
elements.sent-2411-253: In their conventional form, RIS is configured to reflect incoming signals, and therefore, in wireless configurations, the receiver and transmitter are located on the same side of the RIS \cite{10316600,10297571 .}
elements.sent-2411-254: To tackle this limitation, \cite{9437234,9690478 introduced the novel simultaneous transmitting and reflecting RIS (STAR-RIS) concept.}
elements.sent-2411-255: STAR-RIS can provide full space coverage since the incoming signals incident on the STAR-RIS could be separated into reflected and transmitted signals \cite{9570143,10297571 .}
elements.sent-2411-256: To realize the benefits of STAR-RISs, \cite{9462949 analyzed the fundamental coverage characterization.}
elements.sent-2411-257: Currently, no existing analyses focus on EMI-aware STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems.
elements.sent-2411-258: This includes the absence of the analysis of performance limits and the introduction of design guidelines for STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems suffering from EMI.
elements.sent-2411-259: Moreover, as far as we know, the study of STAR-RIS/RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO with phase errors has also been limited with only \cite{10841966 introducing the non-negligible phase error effect on system performance.}
elements.sent-2411-260: Although EMI has been studied in RIS-assisted networks and phase errors have been introduced in STAR-RIS-assisted networks, STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO presents a different landscape due to the specific nature of STAR-RIS, the unique interaction of two technologies, and unavoidable impairment factors.
elements.sent-2411-261: This novel landscape introduces specific system variables, poses system and beamforming challenges, and displays a different problem structure\cite{10556753 .}
elements.sent-2411-262: Motivated by these observations, it is critical to analyze EMI and phase errors jointly to determine the feasibility of deploying STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems, introducing performance limits and design guidelines to promote realistic 6G implementations \cite{10167480,10373089 .}
elements.sent-2411-263: To the best of our knowledge, this paper is the first to focus on the performance analysis of spatially correlated STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems, where STAR-RISs are non-ideal, experiencing EMI and phase errors.
elements.sent-2411-264: In our work, based on the slowly varying statistical channel state information (CSI) in terms of large-scale statistics such as correlation matrices and path-losses\cite{10297571,9875036,10326460 , we introduce a projected gradient descent (GD) algorithm to enhance the channel estimation normalized mean square error (NMSE) and reduce the performance degradation caused by EMI and phase errors.}
elements.sent-2411-265: Subsequently, we derive the data transmission model and the closed-form expressions of the uplink and downlink SE adopting fractional power control.
elements.sent-2411-266: The uplink transmission utilizes the LSFD at the CPU, maximum ratio (MR) and local minimum mean square error (MMSE) combining at APs, while the downlink transmission applies conjugate beamforming and regularized zero-forcing (RZF).
elements.sent-2411-267: The major contributions of the work are listed as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-268: We establish a spatially correlated STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO model with EMI and phase errors, where STAR-RIS-assisted channels experience correlated Rician fading.
elements.sent-2411-269: As far as we know, this is the first work to introduce EMI and phase errors in STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO.
elements.sent-2411-270: These non-negligible factors impair system performance and require analysis to provide system limits and design guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-271: We propose a tailored projected GD algorithm for the uplink channel estimation to optimize the STAR-RIS coefficient matrix based on slowly varying statistical CSI, which contains the optimization of the amplitudes and the phase shifts of the STAR-RIS.
elements.sent-2411-272: Our proposed GD algorithm, tailored to STAR-RIS-assisted networks, can minimize NMSE and introduce an additional channel estimation accuracy gain, addressing the unique challenges of STAR-RIS tied to EMI and phase errors.
elements.sent-2411-273: We develop both uplink and downlink data transmission models experiencing EMI and phase errors.
elements.sent-2411-274: MR and local MMSE combiners are invoked for uplink transmission with a tailored LSFD in the CPU.
elements.sent-2411-275: Conjugate beamforming and RZF precoding are invoked for downlink transmission.
elements.sent-2411-276: Based on the MR combiner and conjugate beamforming, closed-form expressions of uplink and downlink SE are derived with corresponding fractional power control, revealing insights into how EMI and phase errors impact system behaviour, facilitating efficient and targeted STAR-RIS-assisted system design.
elements.sent-2411-277: We demonstrate that the proposed projected GD algorithm can effectively tackle EMI and phase errors to improve estimation accuracy and compensate for performance degradation with nearly $30\%$ NMSE improvement and over $10\%$ SE improvement.
elements.sent-2411-278: Meanwhile, increasing the number of APs, antennas per AP, and STAR-RIS elements can also improve SE performance.
elements.sent-2411-279: Moreover, STAR-RISs cannot bring significant gain when EMI and phase errors are severe, and the STAR-RIS is more susceptible to phase errors.
elements.sent-2411-280: Notably, applying STAR-RIS can achieve better performance in cell-free massive MIMO systems and experience less performance degradation in highly impaired environments compared to conventional RISs.

elements.figure-2411-046: \includegraphics[width=0.76\columnwidth]{RIS_elements_renew.eps} \caption{Sum SE versus the number of STAR-RIS elements $L$ operating at different EMI powers and ranges of phase errors with LSFD and $M=10$, $N=4$, $K=6$, $K_t=K_r=3$ (Analytical Results).} \label{fig_6}
elements.sent-2411-429: It is evident that increasing the number of STAR-RIS elements could markedly enhance system performance.
elements.sent-2411-430: Notably, with the same system configurations, the STAR-RIS can outperform cRIS by around $15\%$-likely SE improvement when $L=16$.
elements.sent-2411-431: However, when $50<L<100$, the performance of STAR-RIS and cRIS is comparable.
elements.sent-2411-432: When $L>100$, STAR-RIS increasingly outperforms cRIS, achieving a nearly $6\%$-likely SE improvement when $L=256$.
elements.sent-2411-433: Meanwhile, when $a=\pi/8$, $\rho=0$ dB introduces an extra $2\%$-likely SE decrease for cRIS than STAR-RIS, compared to $\rho=20$ dB.
elements.sent-2411-434: Similarly, when $\rho=20$ dB, $a=\pi/2$ introduces an extra $7\%$-likely SE decrease for cRIS than STAR-RIS, compared to $a=\pi/8$.
elements.sent-2411-435: Therefore, cRIS is more sensitive to severe EMI and phase errors than STAR-RIS under the proposed scenarios, experiencing higher performance degradation accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-436: These highlight the benefits of introducing STAR-RIS in the severe EMI-aware and phase error-aware environment.
elements.sent-2411-437: Additionally, the STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO offers at least a $20\%$-likely SE improvement over the RIS-free cell-free massive MIMO, motivating the deployment of STAR-RISs in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-438: Meanwhile, when EMI and phase errors are severe ($a=\pi/2$, $\rho=0$ dB), the application of STAR-RISs with the equal coefficient matrix when $L<50$ will underperform conventional cell-free massive MIMO systems.
elements.sent-2411-439: Our projected GD algorithm can help to mitigate this performance degradation and introduce a more than $13\%$-likely SE improvement compared to the equal coefficient matrix.
elements.sent-2411-440: Although severe EMI and phase errors can mitigate the benefits of introducing an increasing number of STAR-RIS elements, the projected GD algorithm consistently enhances performance under various EMI powers ($a=\pi/8$), surpassing the error-free performance with the equal coefficient matrix by at least $6\%$-likely SE improvement when $L=256$.
elements.sent-2411-441: Thus, to fully exploit the benefits of STAR-RIS deployment in real-world applications, efficient coefficient matrix design and other advanced techniques are crucial to address practical impairments, including EMI elimination and phase error compensation methods.
elements.sent-2411-442: To our knowledge, this work is the first to analyze the performance of spatially correlated STAR-RIS-assisted cell-free massive MIMO systems, considering the impacts of EMI and phase errors while conducting SE performance analysis.
elements.sent-2411-443: We introduced the projected GD algorithm for designing the STAR-RIS coefficient matrix to minimize the NMSE.
elements.sent-2411-444: We derived the closed-form expressions of the uplink and downlink SE with respective uplink LSFD processing and downlink conjugate beamforming for performance evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-445: The results reveal that EMI and phase errors cause non-negligible channel estimation accuracy and sum SE degradation.
elements.sent-2411-446: Our proposed projected GD algorithm can introduce nearly $ 30\%$-likely estimation accuracy improvement and over $ 10\%$-likely SE improvement.
elements.sent-2411-447: Increasing the number of APs, antennas per AP and STAR-RIS elements can reduce performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-448: Severe EMI and phase errors could mitigate the advantages of increasing STAR-RIS elements, which the projected GD algorithm can compensate for.
elements.sent-2411-449: Moreover, compared to conventional RISs, STAR-RIS achieves a larger $15\%$-likely SE improvement and exhibits a smaller performance degradation in highly impaired environments, underscoring the necessity of deploying STAR-RISs in cell-free massive MIMO with proper system configurations.
elements.sent-2411-450: Inspired by these results, our future work will focus on scalable implementation and realistic scenarios, such as time-varying channels and user mobility.
elements.sent-2411-451: Furthermore, advanced channel estimation schemes, inter-user interference and EMI mitigation methods will be developed to realize the advantages of deploying STAR-RISs in the cell-free massive MIMO to provide feasible service to the ever-increasing number of users.. \ifCLASSOPTIONcaptionsoff \fi \begin{appendices.}
